dialogues: In Warmup, you saw something important. Same data, but the scale-of-5 graph needed fewer symbols. That's because each symbol represents MORE.
Same data, fewer symbols. That's why scale of 5 is useful when numbers get bigger. What does each symbol represent when the scale is 5?
Right. Each symbol represents 5 items. That's the scale.
Let's see how 20 items become symbols when the scale is 5. Watch. We'll group by 5s.
5, 10, 15, 20. That's 4 groups of 5.
20 items equals 4 symbols. Each symbol shows 5.
It's easier to read this picture graph if you can count by 5s. That's exactly what we do with scale of 5.
Let's check: does the picture graph match our data table? 4 symbols, scale of 5. Count by 5s with me. 4 symbols, counting by 5s. What's the total?
That's it. 20 items. 5, 10, 15, 20. Skip-counting tells you the total in the picture graph and that matches the value in the data table.
Slides has 15. How many symbols is that?
Think: count by 5s until you reach 15. How many 5s? Click to set the height of the Slides.
Right. 3 symbols. 5, 10, 15. You used skip-counting to figure it out.
Monkey Bars has 25. That's a bigger number. Click to set the height of the Monkey Bars.
Right. 5 symbols for 25. You counted: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25.
Let's complete the picture graph. Sandbox has 10. That's the smallest number. Click to set the height for Sandbox.
That's it. 2 symbols for 10. Count by 5s: 5, 10.
You made a scaled picture graph. Let's read it. Which activity is the most popular?
That's right. Monkey Bars has 5 symbols, that's 25. The tallest column has the most votes.
And which got the fewest?
Yes. Sandbox has only 2 symbols, that's 10 votes.
You made a picture graph with scale of 5. Now let's see another way to show the exact same data.
You've read bar graphs before. Here's the same Playground data as a bar graph.
In a picture graph, you count by 5s using symbols: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25.

In a bar graph, you count by 5s using the numbers written along the side of the graph on the axis. Look: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30. Same skip-counting, just written on the side. Both graphs count by 5s. Where do you see the 5s on the bar graph?
Right. The axis shows the skip-counting: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30. That's your scale of 5, built right into the side of the graph.
In a bar graph, you read the height, where the bar ends on the axis. Look at Slides. What's the height of the Slides bar? Where does it end?
Right. The bar's height is 15. It ends at 15 on the axis. Same as 3 symbols on the picture graph. Both show 15.
Before you create the bars, look at the numbers on the axis.
These numbers count by 5s—just like you did with symbols. The axis does the skip-counting for you. What comes after 20 on this axis?
That's it. 25. The axis counts by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30... Same skip-counting you used for symbols, just written on the side.
Pizza has 20 votes. You found 20 on the axis. Set the height for Pizza to show 20.
Right. The bar ends at 20, right on the axis. The skip-counting is already there. You just match the height.
Finish the graph. Salad has 15, Pasta has 25. Set the heights for Salad and Pasta.
Nice. You built the bar graph. Each bar shows exactly how many votes that food got.
Here's the same data as a picture graph. Let's check: do they match? Look at Pasta. The bar shows 25. How many symbols should the picture graph have?
That's right. 5 symbols. Count by 5s: 5, 10, 15, 20, 25. There are 5 fives. Bar graph and picture graph: same information, different format.
You already know how to compare using graphs. Same skill works here.
Right. 15 more. Tacos has 30, Salad has 15. The difference is 15.
So far, some parts of the graphs were already filled in for you. Now let's see if you can build a whole graph on your own.
This graph goes sideways—horizontal. Same idea, you just click for the length for the number of symbols instead of height.
Make a picture graph. Start with Liam. He read 35 books. Set the number of symbols for Liam.
That's it. 7 symbols for 35 books. 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35.
Finish the picture graph. Use the data table and complete the graph.
Nice. You built the whole graph. Each symbol shows 5, and you used skip-counting to figure out how many.
Use your graph to find how many FEWER books Emma read than Olivia.
That's it. Olivia read 40. Emma read 20. The difference is 20.
You built this graph with scale of 5. But what would it look like with a different scale? Try it.
Olivia went from 8 symbols to 40. That's a lot of symbols. Now try scale of 2.
Fewer symbols, but still more than scale of 5. Toggle back to 5.
Same 40 books for Olivia every time. Scale of 1: 40 symbols. Scale of 2: 20 symbols. Scale of 5: just 8.
Right. Scale of 5. Bigger scale, fewer symbols, same data.
All three showed the same information. The scale just changes how many symbols you need. Now let's use your graph to answer some questions.
This bar graph is horizontal. The bars go sideways. But look at the axis along the bottom. Same skip-counting by 5s.
Right. 40. The axis counts by 5s: 5, 10, 15... 35, 40, 45. Same pattern, just running sideways.
Now a bar graph, also horizontal. Read the data, set the lengths of the bars to complete the graph.
Nice. You created the bar graph independently. Horizontal or vertical, you've got this.
How many minutes of screen time on Saturday and Sunday IN ALL?
Right. 65 minutes in all. Saturday has 35, Sunday has 30. 35 plus 30 is 65.
You made both kinds of graphs today. Here's one last question. Picture graphs use symbols. Bar graphs use bars. What do these graphs have in common?
Right. Both use scale of 5. Picture graphs show it with symbols. Count by 5s. Bar graphs show it on the side. Same scale, different format.
You made picture graphs and bar graphs, both with scale of 5. You used skip-counting to figure out symbols and bar heights. Let's see what you know.
